• Next step is sending demo videos for CM, SR, SA, eProc, eInvoicing plus EDI and credentialing — feature- and compliance-focused, not AI-led.
• Demo scheduled 5/20/26 with a $70k-$120k budget estimate pending scope — pricing conversation is active but AI excitement hasn't surfaced.

sources: Avoma demo call 3 June 2026, buyer questions on firewall issues, item search, reassignment
Avoma 3 June, buyer stated 60% own paper / 40% vendor paper ratio
metrics.narrative: Pain points around contract workflow inefficiencies were surfaced and confirmed on the 3 June demo call, but no quantified financial or operational impact has been articulated by the buyer. Specific pain areas identified include: firewall restrictions hindering email-based contract communications with vendors (asked by the buyer on the call: 'we've had firewall issues... if somebody sees an email from an odd vendor'), difficulty locating contracts by item number across amendments and master agreements ('if we typed in a part number... it would return the contract... if it was found on multiple amendments'), challenges coordinating redlining and review workflows among multiple reviewers, and the need to reassign contract ownership when a user exits the organization. The buyer also confirmed a 60/40 split between their own contract templates versus vendor paper, indicating moderate reliance on third-party contract formats that require heavier review. However, none of these pain points have been quantified in terms of cost, time savings, error reduction, or headcount impact. The next move is to anchor these operational challenges to measurable business metrics (FTE time spent on manual contract search, vendor communication delays, risk exposure from lost contract commitments) in a discovery or ROI-focused follow-up conversation.

sources: SF Contact Roles: Shawn Grams (Evaluator, buyer-side) not on any call
Avoma 3 June: operational attendees gave positive feedback but no champion behaviors observed
SF Next Step 5 June: buyer stated 'circle back internally,' no internal advocate named
champion.narrative: No champion has been identified. The deal has one buyer-side contact role (Shawn Grams, Manager Clinic Supply Chain, Evaluator) and one partner contact role (Robert Mayhew, Revo Health, Decision Maker). Shawn Grams has not appeared on any recorded call and has not been cited in any Next Step or task. The 3 June demo was attended by operational users who asked detailed workflow questions and gave positive feedback ('very well done,' 'covered all the areas'), but none of them have been named as contacts, and none have demonstrated champion behaviors (introducing Zycus to senior stakeholders, advocating internally, pushing the process forward, sharing internal docs or timelines). The buyer's statement on 5 June that they need to 'circle back around internally' and that procurement is 'most likely next year' suggests the deal is still in a passive evaluation phase with no internal advocate driving urgency or steering toward a decision. The partner contact (Robert Mayhew, Revo Health) may be facilitating the engagement, but his level of advocacy and influence within Twin Cities Orthopedics is unknown. The next move is to identify and develop a champion from the engaged demo attendees or from senior stakeholders (COO, CFO, or supply chain leadership) by providing value-add follow-up (use-case validation, peer references, ROI modeling) and securing an introduction to the Economic Buyer.

sources: SF Competitors__c field: empty
Avoma 3 June: no competitor mentioned
SF Next Step: no competitor referenced
competition.narrative: No competitor has been named in any Salesforce field, Avoma call, Next Step, or task. The Competitors__c field is empty. On the 3 June demo call, the buyer asked whether the contract module can be deployed standalone and whether it accepts any contract format, which could imply they are evaluating other CLM solutions or incumbent contract management processes, but no specific vendor or alternative was mentioned. The buyer's 60/40 split between their own contract templates and vendor paper suggests they have an existing contract workflow (likely manual or spreadsheet-based, or an incumbent CLM tool), but that incumbent or 'do nothing' baseline has not been named. The buyer's statement that procurement is 'most likely next year' and still 'preliminary' suggests they may not have launched a formal RFP or competitive evaluation yet. The absence of competitive intel is a knowledge gap, not a competitive advantage. The next move is to explicitly ask the engaged contacts (and the partner contact, Robert Mayhew, who may have broader visibility) whether they are evaluating other CLM vendors, what their current contract management process/tool is, and what would drive them to move from that baseline. Until that is confirmed, the competitive landscape is unknown.

sources: Avoma 3 June: buyer stated firewall issues, item search difficulties, reassignment needs, role-based access gaps
Avoma 3 June: buyer confirmed pain points align with Zycus capabilities during demo
identify_pain.narrative: Operational pain has been clearly identified and confirmed by the buyer on the 3 June demo call, though the pain owner (the executive or business leader accountable for fixing it) has not been named. Specific pain points articulated include: (1) firewall restrictions that hinder email-based contract communications with vendors, blocking collaboration and slowing redlining workflows (buyer stated: 'we've had firewall issues... if somebody sees an email from an odd vendor... just thinking what are the steps'), (2) inability to locate contracts by item number across amendments and master agreements, making it difficult to track pricing changes and contract coverage for specific parts (buyer asked: 'if we typed in a part number... it would return the contract... if it was found on multiple amendments'), (3) challenges coordinating redlining and review workflows among multiple reviewers, with version control and clause-level assignment gaps, (4) need to reassign contract ownership when a user exits the organization, creating continuity risk, and (5) lack of role-based access control by contract type (e.g., physician agreements), exposing sensitive contracts to unauthorized users. These are operational inefficiencies in contract lifecycle management, and the buyer validated that Zycus addresses them during the demo. However, the pain has not been escalated to a business impact level (cost, risk, compliance exposure) or tied to a specific executive sponsor who owns the problem. The next move is to map the pain to a senior operational or financial leader (likely the COO or CFO given the account contact list) and quantify the business impact to create urgency.

sources: Avoma 3 June: buyer asked about standalone deployment, no contracting mechanics discussed
SF Contact Roles: Robert Mayhew (Revo Health) marked Decision Maker, channel role unclear
SF Next Step 5 June: 'Most likely next year' signals distant contracting timeline
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec, and partner/SI involvement have not been discussed or documented. No MSA, SOW, Order Form, legal redline process, security questionnaire, or ERP/systems integration scoping has been raised. The buyer asked on the 3 June demo whether the contract module can be deployed standalone (confirmed yes), which implies they may be planning a phased implementation or a contract-only purchase, but the commercial and paper process to execute that has not been defined. The partner contact role (Robert Mayhew, Revo Health) suggests a potential reseller or implementation partner channel, but whether Revo Health will be the contracting party, whether they handle procurement on behalf of Twin Cities Orthopedics, and what that means for the paper process is unknown. No Current Contract Expiration date or incumbent vendor forcing function exists in Salesforce. The buyer's statement that procurement is 'most likely next year' implies contracting would not begin until 2027, but the steps to get there (legal, InfoSec, procurement approval) are unmapped. The next move is to confirm the procurement channel (direct vs. partner-led), map the legal and InfoSec review requirements, and establish the paper process timeline once budget and timing are confirmed.

sources: SF Next Step 5 June: 'need to allocate time and budget. Most likely next year'
SF Contact Roles: Robert Mayhew marked Decision Maker but is partner-side (revohealth.com)
Avoma 3 June: no EB or C-level participation, operational users only
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer has been identified or engaged in any recorded conversation. Budget authority and approval structure are unknown. The buyer explicitly stated on 5 June that they 'need to allocate time and budget' and that procurement is 'most likely next year,' but the person or role who controls that budget decision has not surfaced. The one contact role marked Decision Maker is Robert Mayhew (Vice President Supply Chain, revohealth.com domain), who is a PARTNER/third-party role (Revo Health), not a direct Twin Cities Orthopedics buyer-side executive. No CFO, COO, or C-level buyer stakeholder has joined a call, been named in notes, or been mapped in contact roles. The 3 June demo was attended by mid-level operational users (contract/supply chain managers) who asked detailed workflow and configuration questions but did not discuss budget, approval thresholds, or commercial authority. The evidence suggests procurement timing and budget are controlled at a level above the current engaged contacts, and that level has not yet been reached. Identifying and engaging the Economic Buyer—likely the CFO or COO given the account-level contact list includes a CFO and COO—is the highest-priority move to validate budget, timing, and commercial feasibility.

sources: Avoma 3 June: buyer stated 'circle back around internally and discuss'
SF Next Step 5 June: 'Still preliminary, need to allocate time and budget. Most likely next year'
SF Contact Roles: Robert Mayhew (partner, Revo Health) marked Decision Maker, role unclear
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is still forming and has not been explicitly mapped by the buyer. The deal is in early Qualified stage with two demos completed (20 May and 3 June). On 3 June the buyer stated the next step is to 'circle back around internally and discuss... find out what the rest of team is thinking,' indicating an internal review and consensus-building phase is underway but not yet structured. No formal evaluation timeline, RFP/RFI process, vendor selection committee, or approval chain has been disclosed. The buyer also stated procurement is 'most likely next year,' pushing any formal decision process into 2027. The partner (Robert Mayhew, Revo Health, marked Decision Maker contact role) may play a facilitation or advisory role, but his specific influence and whether Revo Health is driving the process versus Twin Cities Orthopedics directly is unclear. The 3 June demo attendees were operational/mid-level users (contract and supply chain managers), not decision-makers, suggesting the deal has not yet escalated to executive-level evaluation. No multi-stage process (shortlist, finalist, POC, reference checks, commercial negotiation) has been defined. The next move is to map the internal review process with the engaged contacts: who else needs to see the solution, what internal sign-off gates exist, when the budget and timing decision will be made, and whether a formal RFP or competitive evaluation is planned.

sources: Avoma 3 June: buyer questions on item search, reassignment, role-based access, standalone deployment
Avoma 3 June: buyer validated AI features (discovery, Insta Review, Obligation Extraction, Search Genie)
Avoma 3 June: buyer feedback 'very well done' and 'covered all the areas'
decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's evaluation criteria have been partially surfaced through the 3 June contract demo and are grounded in specific operational workflow requirements. Key confirmed criteria include: (1) ability to search contracts by item number across all contract item lists and amendments to identify which contracts contain specific parts, (2) capability to reassign contracts and associated records when users leave the organization to maintain continuity, (3) restrict user access by contract type (e.g., physician agreements) using configurable role-based permissions, (4) deploy the contract management module as a standalone system independent of other S2P modules, (5) accept any contract format (Word, PDF) for AI-powered discovery and risk review, and (6) support email-based and portal-based vendor collaboration without firewall friction. The buyer also validated interest in AI-driven features during the demo: contract discovery via OCR, Merlin Insta Review for clause risk assessment, Obligation Extraction Genie for post-signature commitment tracking, and Search Genie for natural language queries. The demo was well-received ('very well done,' 'covered all the areas'), and the buyer confirmed these capabilities align with their needs. However, formal scoring criteria, weighted priorities, or a documented RFP/RFI have not been shared. The next step is to formalize these into a written evaluation framework and confirm whether other vendors are being assessed against the same criteria.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI Curious. No AIS score in Salesforce, but buyer demonstrated interest in AI capabilities during the 3 June demo. Validated alignment with AI-powered contract discovery (OCR metadata extraction), Merlin Insta Review (AI clause risk grading), Obligation Extraction Genie (AI commitment tracking), and Search Genie (natural language contract search). Buyer asked detailed questions about these features and confirmed they address operational pain points. However, no senior-level AI appetite signal (EB, CFO, or C-level validation), no AI-as-differentiator discussion, and no AI ROI or business-case articulation. Products in scope include Merlin. Positioning is operationally strong but has not been elevated to strategic or commercial urgency.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 13 April 2026, qualified immediately. First demo delivered 20 May (module demo). Preliminary pricing sent 30 May. Second demo (contract-focused) delivered 3 June with strong buyer engagement and positive feedback. Buyer stated on 5 June that demos were 'viewed as great from the team' but deal is 'still preliminary, need to allocate time and budget. Most likely next year.' No stage progression since creation (74 days in Qualified). Last activity 3 June (23 days ago). Rep reached out 5 June with summary; awaiting buyer reply on internal review and next steps.

confidence_signals.summary: Mixed signals. Positive operational engagement (two demos, detailed questions, positive feedback 'very well done' and 'covered all the areas') but passive commercial momentum (budget not allocated, procurement deferred to 2027, no EB engagement, no champion, no formal decision process). Buyer stated on 5 June they will 'circle back internally' with no follow-up in 23 days. Rep reached out 5 June; awaiting buyer reply. Deal is live but cooling due to lack of senior stakeholder engagement and deferred timing.

north_star_verdict.math: 292 days to close from 26 June 2026. Deal created 13 April 2026, currently 74 days old. In Qualified stage with no formal evaluation or shortlist submission dates recorded. Last buyer touchpoint was the contract demo on 3 June 2026 (23 days ago). Buyer feedback on 5 June was explicitly 'Still preliminary, need to allocate time and budget. Most likely next year,' signaling procurement timing is pushed to 2027. At Qualified stage with 292 days remaining, the deal has ample calendar time, but the buyer's own timeline ('most likely next year') implies a real start in Q1 2027 at earliest, compressing the true active cycle to approximately 3-4 months against a typical 12-15 month enterprise motion. No stage progression velocity to measure yet (no Formal Eval or Shortlisted dates). The close date is a placeholder, not buyer-confirmed.
evidence: Buyer explicitly stated 'Still preliminary, need to allocate time and budget. Most likely next year' on 5 June 2026 (Next Step)
Both demos (20 May and 3 June) received positive feedback: 'Viewed as great from the team' and 'very well done' (Next Step, Avoma 3 June)
Preliminary pricing sent 30 May, contract demo delivered 3 June with detailed use-case coverage (Next Step, Avoma)
Deal is 74 days old in Qualified stage with no buyer-confirmed close timeline
Typical enterprise motion is 12-15 months; buyer timing signals Q1 2027 start at earliest, compressing active cycle to 3-4 months before April 2027 close
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal remains stalled 23 days post-demo with no buyer follow-up. Buyer explicitly deferred budget and timing to 2027, signaling loss of urgency. No champion identified, competitive landscape unmapped, and no stage progression in 74 days at Qualified.

flags: Develop a champion from the engaged demo attendees or secure an introduction to supply chain or operations leadership who can advocate internally, drive the evaluation process, and provide visibility into the decision timeline and competitive landscape. The buyer's 'circle back internally' signal requires a champion to ensure momentum does not stall.
best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage deal with strong product fit but critical power and urgency gaps. Buyer engagement is positive but passive (no internal advocate, no EB access, procurement deferred to 2027). Multi-threading is weak (1 buyer-side contact role, none on calls). Competitive landscape is unmapped. Best practices focus on converting operational fit into executive sponsorship, mapping the buying committee and competitive shortlist, and securing a champion to drive internal momentum before the deal goes dormant.

competitive_position.summary: No competitor identified. Competitive landscape is unknown. The buyer has not named any other CLM vendor, incumbent tool, or alternative being evaluated. The buyer asked on 3 June whether the contract module can be deployed standalone, which may imply they are evaluating other solutions or planning a phased approach, but no specific rival has surfaced. The absence of competitive intel is a knowledge gap, not a competitive advantage. Until the buyer discloses whether they are running a formal evaluation, what their current contract management process/tool is, or whether other vendors are in play, the competitive threat level cannot be assessed. The next move is to explicitly map the competitive landscape by asking the engaged contacts and the partner (Robert Mayhew, Revo Health) whether other CLM vendors are being considered, what the current baseline is (manual, spreadsheet, incumbent tool), and what would drive a change.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Strong AI positioning during the 3 June contract demo. Buyer validated interest in and alignment with Zycus AI-driven features: contract discovery via OCR (auto-extract metadata), Merlin Insta Review (clause-by-clause risk assessment against legal playbooks), Obligation Extraction Genie (post-signature commitment tracking), and Search Genie (natural language contract queries). Products in scope include iContract, iSupplier, and Merlin. Demo feedback was explicitly positive ('very well done'). AI capabilities directly address the buyer's pain points (manual contract search, firewall collaboration issues, version control gaps). However, AI fit has not been elevated to a business-case or ROI discussion with senior leadership (EB or CFO). The positioning is operationally strong but commercially unanchored.

Demo feedback was explicitly positive ('very well done,' 'covered all the areas'). However, fit on budget, timing, and business-case ROI is unconfirmed. The buyer stated procurement is 'most likely next year' and they need to 'allocate time and budget,' signaling that fit on commercial feasibility and urgency is still to be established.

value.quote: What do you consider a contract? If you have one contract and you have multiple amendments, are they considered individual entries towards what the license would be, or is it all considered one?
value.requirement: Clarify what constitutes a contract: whether multiple amendments count as individual entries or a single contract for licensing purposes

packets.subject: Clarify what constitutes a contract: whether multiple amendments count as individual entries or a single contract for licensing purposes

value.deliverable: Provide clarification by end of day on contract amendment counting for licensing (whether amendments are individual entries or part of one contract)
value.grounding_quote: Let me go back for that, and I can figure that out. I should be able to have that answer for you by the end of the day.
